sentences.text: Where I was in the-- I was in the-- almost in my house.
I was take a look what happened my house.
And Warsaw was bombarded with merciless-- without mercy.
They see the houses crumble.
People running like crazy from [? our ?] house to another.
Our section was better off because was a Polish section.
The Jewish section was destroyed seat-- one street by another street, and so it was a terrible situation.
Is no water, no food, no nothing.
People running like crazy, asking question [? and answer. ?] We was on the station.
On the station-- we-- we located on the station, they call this [POLISH].
And [? find ?] this [POLISH] was trains, but they go out east, to east Poland, to the border.
So we go to-- they tell us to go to the trains, and the trains with ammunition, they started to go back to this point on Bug, to the Bug, to the Bug.
My family was in the-- in-- in the house.
Yeah, in the house.
And what I remember, what I know, this all families living in-- in Warsaw, they was a special-- special order from the Germans that I find [? up ?] and put in all these people to the ghetto.
This a couple months later.
This is what I know, because I-- I tell [? him ?] the story how this happened I-- because I was in the-- I was in the train, and the train go with ammunition, went to Bug.

And it was bombarded, and-- and was-- it was in bad shape because they-- they-- they-- with the-- with this airplane, what they got, they go down, and with the Stukas, and they-- and they-- they-- they-- they kill a lot of people, anyway, in this-- in this-- in the-- in this-- in this trains.
Yeah, soldier in mixed civilian.
And what-- they had no time to put on this-- this-- this uniforms, so was a mixture that they got the papers to go over there.
They must go to the point.
So we came to-- to one point when the Germans find [? up, ?] the train is loaded with ammunition.
If it's all kind of things, they start to bombard us.
And they got spies or something.
They bombard this-- this train.
In the front of us, was loaded with ammunition.
And this train start to explode.
And it explode for four, five, six hours, and start to be night.
We don't see nothing.
And we stay stationary, and this-- this all things bump-- this all things was bumped out, and five hours we don't see nothing.
We don't-- can't go work and go out.
There's no place to go out.
We were soldiers.
We must stay in place.
And on this engineer of this train, the [?
Bug ?] train, find a way to back up and start to be a little lighter.
Then they cannot bombard us because this whole-- this [? whole ?] air was with dust.
Before it could go down, take a couple hours.
They ?] only got time to back up on the sideline, and we came to a point to Bug, to the Bug.
In the Bug, they start on this-- it's a place I remember.
The place is Rowne, I think.
Rowne or some other place.
I don't remember.
It-- ?] it was in-- in east Poland.
We ?] was thinking-- we start to form something, and was thinking that-- we [? can-- ?] we got some help.
And all of a sudden, we see the help.
Start to came the Russian army.
We don't know [? that ?] they got a treaty between Poland-- between Germany and Russia, because they divided Poland to the Bug, to this River Bug.
We don't know nothing.
And all of a sudden we were thinking, can help?
And this not what's help.
This is Russian army, and they give us plenty fire.
[LAUGHS] The planes came, the Russian planes, bombard us.
I run away like a crazy, throw away everything, and I run.
It's a one story.
[LAUGHS] [?
This story. ?]
I run to hide someplace.
So I run to the place, they call this-- a small place.
It's a-- it's a Jewish shtetl called [NON-ENGLISH].
So I run over there, and I hiding in some places over there.
I find some Jewish, and they hide me in some-- in some place, and I stay over there hiding.
So when I--
Yeah, I throw it away.
Throw away everything-- my arms, everything [? junk. ?] [LAUGHS] Not so-- I'm not no more a soldier, not so nothing.
And I stay over there for a time being.
And then I see through the holes where they hide me, I see is coming the army, the Russian army coming and coming.
For day and night they walking, day and night.
We all [INAUDIBLE].
In this-- was like a barn or something, which were broken this.
You can see through these things, through the holes, that the Russian army passed by.
Yeah.
It was other people over there, too.
Who can remember people hiding?
I was hiding, too.
[LAUGHS] And they walking, and the Russian army take it over.
And that's done.
When this all settled down, I tried to go back to Poland.
I go [INAUDIBLE] back to Poland.
I go back to Poland through the Bug River, and I see what the Germans do to the Jews.
I was in the Warsaw, and see the-- my-- the-- my father, [HEBREW], work on the streets.
Because he got the [? beard, ?] a beard, and some German pass by, knock him off [? with the-- ?] this over the head, with the-- with the-- with the-- with this [? carbine ?] they got.
So-- Then I run back the other side.
I see this terrible story, go back to-- on the Russian side.
No.
I got on ?] the way, but it's no use.
I see what happened.
What I see, I must-- I must do something to help myself and save my life, because I see what happened over there [INAUDIBLE].
So I was on the Russian side, and the Russian side, start a new story.
The rest of a family was in the ghetto, in the ghetto.
They put them all in the ghetto.
And then I got some ideas.
People passing by the borders, I know they was in the ghetto.
They suffer, and the ghetto was overcrowded with people.
People dying of hunger, of all kind of things, diseases.
Because they put on a half a million people in a couple streets.
And then they put on the wall, and [? that's ?] [INAUDIBLE].
We got-- one time I got some letter came from Poland, because they make this-- this type of Poland they make like a-- a general-- general government, something is a special-- special place.
And the Jews was without law, without nothing.
People with Jewish face, they don't belong to nobody.
[INAUDIBLE].
My mother, I don't know when they put [? her ?] in the ovens.
I don't know when they put on my father in the ovens.
My cousins and everybody just [INAUDIBLE].
They died, and I-- I know they died in some--
In someplace.
From Warsaw.
From Warsaw they taken automatically [? without ?] and I think this 1941, '42, '43.
They-- they take it into Auschwitz or Treblinka and some other lagers, all kind of lagers what's all over.
And they take them out.
No, they take them out because they-- they make-- they clean up the city from the Jews by-- by law.
They-- they kept put on a law.
That's so the Jews must be go, and they resettle.
They resettle to Jews to some other places.
They resettle them to Treblinka, or to Auschwitz, or just [? thousands ?] to Chelmno, thousand lagers and that's all.
Who knows what happened?
I see just my father, and that's from far away.
And then I ran away.
I would go back, it's no use.
No, no, no.
I don't see.
I don't want to see.
Nobody can go over there because of dangerous.
Dangerous to went into street.
Dangerous everyplace.
A Polack [? can ?] tell me, this is a Jew, they kill you.
No, I wasn't over there.
And I don't wear nothing because they-- I go back the other way, because I see the situation is-- is no solution to the situation.
When I-- I went through this-- because I know the Polish language, and [INAUDIBLE] for me was easy to go through the border.
I go [? across. ?] The borders was loose.
And I went back to Ukraine.
They make this a [? part ?] [INAUDIBLE] Ukraine.
They-- they-- they take from Poland this [INAUDIBLE] and take it just to-- to Russia and that sort.
So it's a Russian territory, [INAUDIBLE].
I don't have special papers.
I got just a paper from-- what I got papers for like a-- not a passport.
Oh, I got a paper-- not a paper.
There was-- in fact, [NON-ENGLISH].
This mean you-- you got a paper where you can drive a car.
A driver's license with a picture.
Yeah.
And I got the driver's license because I drive over there, I got [INAUDIBLE].
I don't have a car.
No, I don't have a car, so I got the-- this license, this driver's license, my picture.
So--
I was working in Poland.
I was working for-- for a French company, Citroen.
And I was working couple years over there, then--
A couple years I work over there, then I make some money extra.
So I work over there, and when I-- when war start-- before the war start-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Anyway, the Germans take away everything.
I got a-- a little business.
I opened a business myself.
I got money, opened a business.
Now the war start, and I was in the army.
And the [INAUDIBLE] [? gone ?] [? away. ?] They take away.
I got a business what got to do with something like this, glass.
Glass and porcelain, and some other items-- luxuries.
Some luxury items.
I like this stuff, and I--
It's not a bazaar.
It's a store.
It's a store with all kind of items.
And you got to do with some items-- we got to do with-- what you got, I need it in the-- in the house?
Not, no, in the house, but you need some instrument, knives and this, and all kind of things besides-- besides this porcelain, all kind of porcelain.
Ceramics, all kind of porcelain.
They like this, and I got the shelves with this.
My own business.
Probably I was about 25 or something. 24, yeah.
So I got my own business.
I make some-- I make money over there, making for Citroen, because I was-- I was a technician in welding, electrical welding.
And they make-- we make the bodies.
The motors came from French, from Paris, some places, and the bodies was done in-- in-- in Warsaw, all bodies by specification.
I was the technician on this because everything was builded by electric welding and some other type.
So I [? finished ?] some special [? school ?] for welding.
So-- so this was my specialty, technician in welding, electric, and some other things.
So--
After I was in Ukraine, I was hiding.
And they start to hunt for people from Polish, officers and something like this.
They start to hunt for these people, looking around, where to do, what to do.
A lot of people signed-- Jewish people signed they won't go back to the homes.
And on the [INAUDIBLE] there, there'd be Russian [INAUDIBLE] they put on the list of these [? whole ?] people, and they start to people-- take away to Siberia.
They catch me--
They don't give you a Russian [? citizenship. ?] They give you some temporary piece of paper when you can stay over there.
You know, because it's a border, you can stay just 100 kilometer from the border.
You cannot stay on the border, and then they give you a piece of paper.
Now, mostly Jewish people, they don't want to stay because the-- the-- the conditions was so bad, is so terrible, they were thinking they go back to Germany.
They go and go there, they don't know what kind of condition.
I tell the people they kill you.
And anyway, they go, they-- and they-- they won't go back.
So the [INAUDIBLE] they take them at nighttime.
Take all the Jewish people.
That's ten of thousands, and this [? was luck. ?]
This was in 1930-- this was in 1940, I think.
In every town.
In every town in the border town, in all border towns of Ukraine-- [NON-ENGLISH] Ukraine, they called this.
All the Jewish people, they was taken [? down ?] the night in the-- in the [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH] means the wagons, like for the cattles.
And they take them, and I see where they take them without end.
They take me, too.
And I run away, and I hiding.
They take them away to Siberia, some other place.
I was hiding some places in Rowne.
That place-- Rowne is the city.
I was hiding over there.
And anyway, they catch me, catch me in Rowne.
The [INAUDIBLE] they catch me.
They-- in the special, they catch people what they was in the army.
Luckily, in the army, the Polish army, I wasn't an officer.
I was just-- I got two-- two stripes.
It's not a big deal, anyway.
So when they catch me, they put me in jail with other Polish officers.
You're ?] supposed to [? stay in ?] jail.
[LAUGHS] [INAUDIBLE].
The jail was-- first of all, they interrogate.
They ask you questions.
They check on you.
They put in their finger in the ass and see maybe you got some hidden papers or whatever.
[LAUGHS] And then they cut off your hair.
They take a look that everything's clear.
Check your ears, put down over there, everything's-- no hidings [LAUGHS] places.
So they put me in the-- in a-- in a place like this.
You stay just dark and stay naked.
They take you out, and somebody check you.
Put in a finger here, put a finger here, put a finger in the back, in the ass.
[LAUGHS] Maybe you're hiding something.
I don't have nothing.
And they keep you.
And you lay there, and keep you 24 hours in this place.
They take you to the regular--
It's a box, dark box, no lights.
And then--
You can no-- you stay in the box straight.
After this, they take you out from the box, and they take you, put on the regular jail.
The regular jail, there was a room the half size of this, and it was over there--
From half here to this place, maybe.
A little longer.
Maybe not longer.
It was--
Yeah, something.
It was over there officer-- Polish officers, and I was not officer.
I was soldier.
There was 50 people, officers.
Polish officers.
Later ?] they find out what to do with the people.
I don't know what they do with the people [INAUDIBLE] I was laying with these people, and when--
All Polish.
They don't know I'm a Jew.
Polish-- Polish soldier, [INAUDIBLE] find [? up and ?] got some papers.
I got this-- this-- this paper, I'm in the-- this-- from the-- some paper from Poland, [INAUDIBLE].
They put the physical exam.
They don't care.
They put my physical.
This by routine.
Just do the by routine.
And they ask you questions, and they all kind of things, take it-- talk about this and this.
And they putting a gun to the head, and you must talk about this.
All kind-- and sign papers.
You guilty for this?
When you-- you must sign paper.
You guilty for everything.
You don't sign the paper, guilty, you kaput anyway, so anything what they tell me sign.
I don't know the Russian language good.
I just understand a little bit.
What they tell me sign?
[NON-ENGLISH]?
I-- I-- I sign it, I sign it, I sign it.
I'm guilty for everything.
[LAUGHS] It's OK.
So after this, when I'm guilty for everything, then they can take me out to the regular jail.
The regular jail [INAUDIBLE] was half a room like this.
It was-- 6 by--
Yeah.
And down in the-- in corner there was a [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH] is a-- a little like-- not a kettle.
It's like-- you know, you can sit down on this.
You can just do the-- you can just relieve yourself.
Like a latrine, in the corner, so you can relieve yourself in this.
And for the big things, they opened the door.
You go around, you go out, and they stay-- the guards stay.
And there was a-- a big-- some hole and a piece of wood.
You stay on the wood and make the big things, and they give you one minute.
When you don't do the right things, you can keep the hands back in the-- [LAUGHS] behind you because you don't finish up, you must go back.
So if some people finish, if some people don't finish, you go back.
[NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH].
And once a day, they give you coffee and a piece of bread, every day.
So I was laying on this condition.
There was-- the air was terrible.
We can cut the air with a knife.
This was in 1941.
Time of the year, it was-- I think it was fall.
In the fall, yeah.
So I was laying over there, and we--
And I was thinking about dying because with this kind of food.
And beside this, when you lay down, when you sleep over there, and you [INAUDIBLE] sleep, when one man turn around, everybody must turn around, because no other way.
So when the end turned around, I must turn around this way, or they [INAUDIBLE] turn around this way, because you never sleep on one side.
So I was laying near to another officer, and this was officer from the border police.
He was officer with the name Mr.
Kowalski.
I remember his name exactly.
And it's a funny things with talking always, like what-- what be the end of us.
The officers [INAUDIBLE].
I wasn't an officer, anyway.
This was a Polish officer from the-- from the border police.
Nice fellow, Mr.
Kowalski.
We are talking [INAUDIBLE].
And we're laying around.
This was a couple months already, two months, three months.
They're telling me, how long we can live here?
We're dying anyway here.
Or they take us [? else, ?] we'll be dying here with no food, no air.
So Mr.
Kowalski tell me-- [LAUGHS] I was laying next to him.
He tell me, Mr.
Krause, you know-- I tell him, what you know?
He tell me, I know something what I can tell you.
What he can tell me?
You see, we're dying.
We been-- must die.
No, I want-- I want tell you what your future be here.
I can't tell you for myself.
I have not the power.
Now I got the power-- actually, I was born with this power.
I can tell you you'll be free or you die here, or something.
I tell him [? sound ?] meshugenah.
[LAUGHS]
So this man, we're laying around like this.
I-- I remember to this day.
It's unbelievable.
He take a piece of-- a piece of thread.
He put on a-- on the thread a piece of-- a piece of wood.
He put on the thread like this, and this wood hanging on the thread.
And he put on there four corners of the wood.
He says ?] [?
Mr.
Krause, ?] this is a piece of-- crumb of bread, and this is sand, and this is something.
Four corners of the wood he put on something.
And he tell me, when this go, this piece of wood go on-- on the bread, you be free.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think [? this ?] a Polack [? and ?] meshugenah.
[LAUGHS] [INAUDIBLE] we die here.
He look at me, laying [INAUDIBLE].
He put on [? the ?] [? weight. ?] [?
Things ?] go on the wood-- on the bed.
And again on the [? wood, ?] he look at me.
Mr.
Krause, you be free.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think to myself, [? now ?] a meshugenah.
[LAUGHS] He can't do it-- I cannot do for myself.
I see you, you be free.
The next day, the door was open.
Open the door.
They camed in, [INAUDIBLE] with this, with this [INAUDIBLE], certain other with the list.
And [INAUDIBLE], [NON-ENGLISH], [LAUGHS] [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], Gabriel [?
Wolfovich ?] Krause, stand up!
I stand up, [LAUGHS] and the [NON-ENGLISH].
Mean go, [NON-ENGLISH].
I stand up.
I don't have time to tell him goodbye, and I [NON-ENGLISH].
I open the door.
And I go with these-- these people-- with these two.
One in the front of me, [LAUGHS] [? one with the other. ?] And I go out, and so what was the luck?
I was not a [? first ?] officer.
That's why they let you out?
[INAUDIBLE] [?
I wasn't an ?] officer.
That's what [INAUDIBLE].
He tell me-- he give me prognosis that I supposed to go out.
I go out.
I remember to this day.
I go out, and from this moment, some other story start.
I was free.
I was free.
And these officers, because was ready to find out, they killed 12,000 to 15,000 Polish officers.
They put them in all kind of-- all kind of places like [INAUDIBLE] Polish.
I mean like-- like the Germans make special places for this.
They keep these officers, and they start in 1941-- and I forget the date.
All of a sudden, they got a treaty-- Poland got a treaty with Germany.
No, not Poland.
I mean Russia, with the German got a treaty for 20 years.
And all of a sudden one day, they start a war.
And this all Polish officer was concentrated in-- in Ukraine, some places on the borders, and it came a special [NON-ENGLISH].
This mean a-- a special-- special thing for Stalin.
And [NON-ENGLISH] Polish lagers.
[NON-ENGLISH] These all Polish officers got a-- a bullet in the head, and goodbye Charlie.
They kill them all.
And then they claim the-- the Germans do it.
They do this, and cover the-- kill them. 12 to 15.
It was 300 Jewish officers over there, in Galicia.
They kill them, too.
So I was lucky I wasn't over there, because I wasn't an officer.
So you were not a prisoner anymore?
I went out, and then I start to go-- they-- when they went out, they-- they-- I must do something.
Must have some-- someplace to catch up with something.
This was in-- this was in Rowne, in [NON-ENGLISH], Ukraine.
They call it [NON-ENGLISH], Ukraine.
This was in Rowne.
So I tried to do something for myself to see a way out someplace, and in the war start-- They gave you papers when they let you out?
Yeah, they give me a-- a paper.
They let me out.
A piece of paper, let me out.
My name [INAUDIBLE].
So I must find something to do, something to go out, because this area was a dangerous area.
And-- and when I stay over there, start the war with the-- all of a sudden, start the war.
Start a war with this-- with the-- between Poland-- between Germany and-- and Russia, start a war.
They was official-- not official war.
All of a sudden, they bombard everything, and I start to run away someplaces.
And I run away-- I remember I run away.
And when the war start, I run about 300 miles. 300 miles or more.
And this place-- I remember this place, Wlodzimierz Wolynski.
This was a place, and I walk, walk over there, day and night, to this place, and over there--
Wlodzimierz Wlodzimierz, they call this Wlodzimierz Wolynski.
This was a place where they got over there-- like the-- the Russian army got over there like special-- not post.
More like post over there.
Like you cannot go in because it was everything [NON-ENGLISH].
This means mines, all over is mines, so I cannot go in.
Now I got this piece of paper.
I show this piece of paper [INAUDIBLE].
Then the soldier show up, a soldier, and then I go into the place, to the city.
The other way I cannot go because I'd be dead.
Yeah, that-- that I can go in.
I go in, and the soldiers [? show me. ?] I says, the soldiers, I show them this paper.
[NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]
So I [INAUDIBLE] because he tell me I'd be dead, because I don't know how to go in.
Go, go, go, go, and I went into the city, Wlodzimierz Wolynski.
And from this city, I see this-- this situation was-- because I hear-- this situation was so fluid the whole Russian army was destroyed in couple days.
They catch over there [INAUDIBLE] always-- over a million-- a million Russian army destroyed.
I hear this some-- some-- came some news.
And I think to myself, well, must save myself and go away from the situation.
So I catch a train.
Train go and come.
I catch a train.
No money.
You don't need the money.
Just-- just go and catch up with the train, outside, inside.
You go in this way.
I catch up, and I go night and day.
I wind up in Kiev, a big city in Ukraine.
A big city, beautiful city, with a lot of Jews over there.
So I went over there on mark-- on the market, and Jews over there.
I tell them, [SPEAKING YIDDISH].
They look at me, [?
I'm a ?] meshugenah [YIDDISH].
[LAUGHS] He came from-- came from Warsaw.
Here, he telling me [YIDDISH].
Our [SPEAKING NON-ENGLISH] army, this the biggest power in the world.
[LAUGHS]
Yeah.
Yeah.
That--
